description: Nearly indestructible broad-leaved rhizome plant. Attach to wood or rock — burying the rhizome rots it. Tolerates low light and grazing fish.
Compact form of Anubias barteri, perfect for foreground hardscape. Slow but extremely hardy; thrives attached to driftwood in low light.
Classic beginner plant with long leathery leaves. Attach the rhizome to hardscape; reproduces from plantlets on leaf tips. Unfussy about water.
Lace-tipped cultivar of Java fern with finely branched leaf ends. Same bulletproof care: low light, no CO2, attach to wood or rock.
Big classic centerpiece rosette that can dominate a tank's background. Heavy root feeder — give it a nutrient-rich substrate or root tabs.
Tape-like leaves that form a swaying background curtain. Spreads quickly by runners and tolerates hard, alkaline water.
Giant vallisneria whose ribbon leaves trail across the surface of even tall tanks. Excellent nitrate sponge for large setups.
Adaptable crypt available in green, brown and red forms. May melt back after planting but regrows from the roots. Happy in low light.
The smallest crypt — a slow, dense foreground tuft that needs patience but no trimming. Likes more light than its bigger cousins.
Long, ruffled strap leaves that arch over the back of the tank. Tolerates harder water than most crypts.
The do-everything moss: carpets, walls, breeding cover for fry and shrimp. Grows in almost any water and any light.
Triangular fronds that hang like tiny fir branches — tidier than Java moss. Grows slower and appreciates cooler, cleaner water.
Tiny floating discs that double seemingly overnight — a ferocious nitrate sponge that's almost impossible to remove once established.
Floating rosettes like miniature lily pads with long dangling roots fish love to shelter in. Shades the tank and eats nitrates fast.
Floater that blushes deep red under strong light, with striking red roots. Needs calm surface water and bright lighting to color up.
Velvety floating fern with water-repellent leaf hairs. Easier to control than duckweed while doing the same shading and nitrate work.
Rootless bristly stems that grow ridiculously fast floated or anchored. One of the best nitrate sponges in the hobby; sheds needles when adjusting.
Lacy, fast-growing stems that fill a background in weeks. Leaf shape changes with conditions; easy to propagate from cuttings.
The standard aquascaping stem: small round leaves that turn pink-orange near strong light. Trims into dense colored bushes.
Red-undersided stem plant that brings easy color without CO2. More light means deeper reds.
Thick-leaved upright stems that grow slowly and forgive neglect. Crushed leaves smell faintly of lemon and mint.
Feathery fan-leaved stems that look spectacular but demand bright light and clean water; falls apart in dim tanks.
Tiny-leaved stem that can be trimmed into bushes or pinned down as a carpet. Pearls with oxygen bubbles under strong light.
Grassy lawn plant that spreads by runners into a meadow. Needs strong light and a fine substrate to carpet properly.
Round-leaved carpeting plant — the easier alternative to dwarf baby tears. Still wants good light to hug the substrate.
Grass-like rosettes that runner into a low jungle without CO2 — the easiest path to a carpet look. Tolerates hard water.
Jewel-like rhizome plant with iridescent dark leaves. Treat like a fancy Anubias: attach to hardscape, low light, lots of patience.
Transparent-green fern with intricate fronds, happiest attached near gentle flow in soft, slightly acidic water.
Fine lacy fern that grows planted or floating, doubling as fry cover. Fast enough to outcompete algae in new tanks.
Upright succulent-leaved stems that creep toward the light. Slow, tidy and hard to kill — good first stem plant.
Actually a ball of algae — roll it occasionally to keep it round. Prefers cool water and shrugs off near-total neglect.
Quirky plant with banana-like tubers at its base and lily-pad leaves that race to the surface. Rest the tubers on the substrate, don't bury them.
Dramatic red-spotted lily grown from a bulb. Trim surface pads to keep it compact and bushy, or let a few rise for shade.
The classic coldwater-tolerant oxygenator, great for goldfish setups. Grows fast planted or floating and soaks up nutrients.
Velvety floating rosettes with long feathery roots — instant shade and shelter. Dislikes water droplets sitting on its leaves.
Seaweed-looking fern gametophyte that grows in loose translucent ruffles. Slow, undemanding, and loved by shrimp.
Possibly the easiest stem plant alive — grows in almost any light and water, fast enough to need weekly trims. Check local regulations; invasive in some regions.
Miniature sword plant that chains across the foreground by runners, forming a grassy lawn under good light.
Compact creeping stem that forms neat green cushions along the front of aquascapes. Slow and tidy; trim to encourage spreading.
Feathery cabomba look-alike that's far less demanding — grows fast even in modest light. Great beginner background bush.
The classic high-tech carpet — the smallest-leaved aquarium plant. Forms a dense lawn only under strong light with injected CO₂; without CO₂ it melts. Demanding but iconic in aquascaping.
Fast-spreading foreground carpet that hugs the substrate under bright light and CO₂. Grows vertically and traps debris if light or CO₂ is too low — needs regular trimming.
Brilliant deep-red stem plant prized in aquascapes. Holds its colour only under intense light, injected CO₂ and rich dosing; otherwise it fades and drops leaves. One of the more demanding reds.
Compact red stem that stays low enough for the mid-ground. Develops its best pink-to-purple tones under strong light with CO₂; manageable but distinctly a high-tech plant for full colour.

Hardy green crypt with narrow wavy leaves, a touch smaller and tougher than C. wendtii. Thrives in low light without CO₂ and tolerates a wide pH range. Like all crypts it may melt after replanting, then regrows — leave it undisturbed.

Grass-like crypt with long, twisted ribbon leaves that form a tall background thicket. Undemanding in low to moderate light; CO₂ is optional and speeds growth. A good low-tech alternative to Vallisneria.
Small, slow crypt with slender pointed leaves, ideal for a foreground or low midground. Spreads by runners into a dense patch over time. Easy in low light and no CO₂, but slow to establish.
A giant crypt with long, hammered-bronze leaves that make a dramatic background in big tanks. Unusually tolerant of harder, alkaline water. CO₂ deepens the bronze colour but isn't required.
Compact crypt with olive-to-bronze wavy-edged leaves, great for the midground or as a foreground in larger tanks. Suffers less crypt-melt than most when transitioned. Easy in low light without CO₂.
Wavy-leaved crypt that takes on reddish-brown tones under brighter light and CO₂; greener in low-tech tanks. Undemanding and hardy either way, spreading slowly by runners into a midground clump.

Striking hybrid sword whose leaves are mottled deep red and maroon over green. Needs moderate-to-bright light to hold its colour; in low light it reverts to green. A heavy root feeder — give it root tabs. CO₂ optional but boosts colour.

Popular hardy sword with reddish-brown new leaves spotted darker, fading to olive with age. Undemanding and good for beginners given a nutrient-rich substrate. Makes a large background centrepiece in bigger tanks.

Large sword with broad ruby-to-burgundy leaves on red stems, a dramatic focal point. Wants bright light, a rich substrate and iron dosing for best red; CO₂ intensifies the colour. Hardy once established.
Curiosity cultivar with narrow, corkscrew-twisted green leaves that fan out in the midground. Easy and tolerant of a wide range of conditions; spreads by runners. CO₂ speeds growth but isn't needed.

The most beginner-friendly Aponogeton, grown from a bulb into a rosette of long, crinkle-edged translucent leaves. Fast and easy in medium light; may take a dormant rest, dropping leaves before regrowing. CO₂ optional.

Bulb plant that throws out many broad, wavy, lime-green translucent leaves for a billowing background. Fast-growing in moderate light; bright light can invite algae on its big leaves. Takes periodic dormancy.
Sturdier, broader-leaved relative of dwarf sagittaria, forming grassy clumps in the midground. Spreads readily by runners. Stays more compact under brighter light; easy in medium light without CO₂.

Striking background stem whose long, narrow leaves radiate in whorls like octopus tentacles. Wants medium-to-high light to stay compact; leggy and thin without it. A hungry feeder — dose well. CO₂ optional but recommended.

Fine, needle-like leaves in dense upright whorls give it a bright-green conifer look, great for vertical texture. Bright light and CO₂ keep the internodes tight; without CO₂ it grows looser and slower.

Delicate, feathery stem that flushes pink to deep red and purple under intense light and CO₂. Demanding: needs strong light, pressurised CO₂ and stable soft water, or it thins and rots. One for high-tech tanks.

Sought-after rotundifolia form with slender leaves that turn vivid pink, orange and red. The intense colour only comes with strong light, CO₂ and rich dosing; greener and leggy without them. Fast — trim often.
One of the easiest red stems — holds deep blood-red leaves even in medium light, redder still under bright light and CO₂. More forgiving than most reds, but stable parameters matter or it can melt.

Large-leaved, very fast and forgiving background stem — almost unkillable, an excellent beginner plant. Grows well in medium light with no CO₂, faster and lusher with both. Sheds lower leaves if shaded.

Bright-green feathery foxtail with finely divided leaves that make a soft, billowing background. Grows fast; CO₂ keeps it dense and tight, while low-tech tanks get leggier growth. Likes regular dosing and trimming.

Anubias with deeply textured, coffee-coloured young leaves that mature to dark green. Tough, slow epiphyte — attach the rhizome to wood or rock, never bury it. Keep it shaded; bright light invites algae on its slow leaves.

Miniature Anubias with tiny leaves, perfect glued to hardscape in the foreground or on nano driftwood. As hardy and undemanding as full nana but a fraction of the size. Attach the rhizome — don't bury it.

Taller Anubias with long, narrow spear-shaped leaves, useful as a hardy background epiphyte. Same easy care as the rest of the genus — slow, low-light, no CO₂ needed. Attach to wood or rock, leaving the rhizome exposed.

Popular Buce with wavy-edged dark-green leaves and a subtle blue sheen, a colourful Anubias alternative. Very slow epiphyte — attach to hardscape, never bury the rhizome. Easy in low light; CO₂ speeds growth and colour.

Compact, prized Buce with pale-veined olive-brown leaves that stay small enough for the foreground. Slow-growing epiphyte for hardscape; CO₂ brings out richer colour but isn't required. Keep light modest to avoid algae.

Distinctive moss whose shoots grow vertically in twisting, flame-like columns rather than spreading flat. Attach to wood or rock; easy in low to medium light with no CO₂, though CO₂ makes it fuller and faster.

Moss whose fronds droop downward for a soft, weeping look, especially attractive trained over driftwood branches. Easy in low to medium light; CO₂ accentuates the drooping habit and speeds growth but isn't needed.

One of the very few true carpeting plants that forms a lawn without CO₂. Develops small clover-like to single-lobed leaves close to the substrate under good light; grows taller and looser if shaded. Slow but rewarding low-tech carpet.

Grass-like foreground carpet of short, narrow blades that spreads by runners into a dense lawn. Needs bright, direct light to carpet rather than grow tall; CO₂ thickens it. A nutrient-rich substrate helps it establish.

Fast, undemanding bushy plant that can be planted or left to float, prized as fry and shrimp cover and a great nutrient sponge against algae. Thrives in low light with no CO₂ across a wide pH range. Brittle — it propagates from any fragment.

A rootless floating liverwort that aquascapers also tie down with mesh to form a bright, pearling green carpet. Naturally floats; kept submerged it needs strong light and benefits greatly from CO₂. Trim often or it lifts off and floats away.

Fast, easy creeping carpet of tiny three-lobed leaves that spreads across the foreground and climbs hardscape. Stays low and dense under bright light; grows taller in shade. CO₂ speeds it up but isn't required — trim to keep it flat.

Compact, feathery moss that grows in tight deep-green clumps rather than spreading, holding its shape well on wood and rock. Slower than most mosses and a little more light- and CO₂-responsive, but easy in clean, well-oxygenated water.

Unusual fern-like Hygrophila with deeply lobed, bronze-to-brown leaves that can be planted or attached to hardscape like an epiphyte. Stays compact and colourful under good light with CO₂; greener and leggier without. Side-shoots readily.

Tall background stem with narrow leaves that flush copper-orange to pink-red under strong light. Wants bright light and CO₂ for best colour and to keep its lower leaves; pale and bare-stemmed when underlit. Fast and hungry.

Aromatic background stem with green tops and purple undersides that deepen to red-purple under bright light and CO₂. More forgiving than it looks — tolerant of a wide range of water, just leggier in low light. Propagates from cuttings.

Compact, intensely red dwarf macrandra — one of the most demanding reds in the hobby. Needs strong light, stable pressurised CO₂ and soft, acidic water; it sulks at the slightest CO₂ swing. Strictly a high-tech, expert plant.

Slow-to-medium stem whose narrow leaves turn purple-to-pink under strong light, deepest with CO₂ and full dosing. Hardier than most coloured stems and grows green in modest light. A nice colour accent for the midground.

Dwarf cardinal plant with round, bright-green leaves on compact stems, low enough for the foreground. Slow but easy in medium light without CO₂; CO₂ tightens its growth. A tidy, undemanding splash of fresh green.

A more colourful, easygoing rotundifolia form that takes on orange and red tops under strong light. As tough and fast as standard rotundifolia — grows green in low light, no CO₂ needed, brighter with both. A great first 'red' stem.

Distinctive trailing stem with round, kidney-shaped bright-green leaves and a vine-like habit, good for cooler tanks. Prefers cooler water — gets leggy and small-leaved in sustained heat. Easy in medium light without CO₂.

Tiny floating fern that forms a fine green-to-reddish carpet on the surface, reddening under bright light. Very fast — harvest often or it shades everything below. Dislikes strong surface agitation; an excellent nutrient sponge against algae.

Onion-bulb plant with very long, narrow, crinkled dark-green leaves that arch like a curtain from a background corner. Slow but tough and long-lived, and its hard leaves are ignored by plant-eaters. Easy in low light; don't bury the bulb fully.

Famous for leaves that are pure lattice — a skeleton of veins with no tissue between. Striking but fussy: wants clean, well-oxygenated water with flow, moderate light, and a rest period. Prone to algae on the lace and to bulb rot if buried.

Charming low plant with star-shaped rosettes of wavy, curling bright-green leaves. Under strong light with CO₂ it stays low and carpets via side-shoots; in low light it grows taller and looser. Likes stable water — sudden pH swings melt it.

Tall cousin of dwarf hairgrass that throws up long, grassy blades for a flowing background meadow. Spreads by runners; stays a touch shorter under bright light. Easy and undemanding — no CO₂ needed, tolerant of harder water.

Compact, slow Hygrophila with small lance-shaped leaves that flush bronze to reddish-brown under strong light and CO₂. Slower and more colourful than its bigger relatives, suiting a midground accent. Greener and leggier in low light.

Java fern variety with finely divided, multi-pronged leaves that give a feathery, branching look. As hardy and forgiving as standard java fern — attach the rhizome to wood or rock, never bury it. Easy in low light without CO₂.

Slim-leaved java fern that grows at a steeper, tidier angle than the broad type, popular on driftwood. Tough, slow and undemanding — tie the rhizome to hardscape and leave it. Low light, no CO₂; bright light can scorch it.

Bright light-green wendtii form with slightly textured leaves, a fresh midground accent. As easy and adaptable as any wendtii — thrives in low light with no CO₂ and a wide range of water. May melt then regrow after replanting.

Upright, barely-branching stem with narrow leaves in deep red-purple to burgundy. Needs bright light to keep its colour — reverts to dull green when underlit. CO₂ and steady dosing bring out the richest tones. A bold background red.

Fine-leaved rotala that flushes orange and red at the tops under strong light and low nitrates. Bushy and easy to train with trimming; CO₂ improves density and colour but isn't essential. A versatile, vivid background plant.

Compact, slow stem with neat, rounded leaves stacked in tidy layers, almost shrub-like for the midground. Tips turn coppery-red under strong light and CO₂. Can melt if planted straight under water — establish it gently. Rewarding once settled.

Vigorous, undemanding plant with round coin-shaped leaves on vining stems; can be planted, left to trail, or floated. Fast and easy across a wide range of light and water with no CO₂. A great nutrient sponge and fry cover.

Pink-vein form of scarlet temple with rose-coloured leaves veined in cream. Wants strong light, a rich substrate and iron for full colour; CO₂ deepens it. A vivid pink-red contrast for the midground, a little fussier than green stems.

Bulb lily that throws up striking arrow-shaped leaves in deep red, mottled darker under bright light. Will send lily pads to the surface if allowed — trim them to keep it bushy below. Hardy and easy; CO₂ and light only intensify the red.

Fine, feathery underwater water-hyacinth with whorled green leaves. Demanding: it needs strong light and injected CO₂ to build firm stems, turning transparent and shedding leaves in shade. Soft, nutrient-rich water and a high-tech setup are a must.

Dwarf, tightly-leaved form of moneywort that stays low and bushy, good for the midground. Very easy and adaptable across a wide range of light and water with no CO₂; pinch the tips for side-shoots. Slow but trouble-free.

Bushy, grass-like rosette that looks like a tuft of bamboo and bronzes to reddish under strong light. Stays compact in bright light with CO₂; leggy and pale when shaded. Likes soft, acidic water and a nutrient-rich substrate.

Delicate fine-needled stem that tints orange-red at the tips under intense light. Best planted in dense bunches for a bushy effect; needs strong light, CO₂ and iron to colour up and stay healthy. Prefers soft water.

Fast, fine-leaved bushy stem that branches densely under good light. CO₂ keeps it compact and lush; without it the growth turns flaccid and stretched. Easy and vigorous otherwise — trim often to keep it from taking over.

Star-shaped grassy rosette prized in aquascaping but genuinely demanding: it needs intense light, injected CO₂, a rich substrate and very stable, soft, acidic water. Sensitive to sudden changes. Strictly a high-tech, expert plant.

Soft, mossy-looking stem clothed in tiny needle leaves for a feathery background texture. Wants bright light to stay green and compact — the lower stem yellows and dies back if shaded. CO₂ and soft water bring out its best.

Unusual creeping plant with tiny umbrella-like, three-pronged leaves on slender stalks that spreads by runners across the foreground. Stays low and compact under strong light; leaf stalks stretch tall in shade. Pearls beautifully with CO₂.

Distinctive stem whose leaves turn from green to fiery orange and develop saw-toothed serration under strong light and CO₂. Tolerates cooler water, so it suits temperate setups too. Slow-growing; wants soft water and a rich substrate to colour up.

A carnivorous grassy carpet that forms a fine bright-green lawn — its roots bear tiny bladder traps for micro-organisms. Needs strong light, stable injected CO₂ and ideally a matured tank; CO₂ swings and fresh tanks cause it to melt. A high-tech carpet.

Striking stem with whorls of stiff, pale blue-green needle leaves. Genuinely demanding: it requires intense light, injected CO₂, soft acidic water and constant nutrients, never letting nitrate or phosphate hit zero. Strictly an expert plant.

Background stem that glows golden-yellow to orange under strong light, a rare warm contrast to greens and reds. Needs bright light, soft water and good dosing to keep its colour; greener and leggier without CO₂. A heavy feeder.

Fast background stem with whorled leaves that are green above and purple-magenta beneath, deepening under strong light. More forgiving than many colour stems — grows green in moderate light without CO₂, vivid with both. Easy to propagate by cuttings.

Large-leaved green-to-orange macrandra form, hardier than the red type but still high-tech. Wants strong light, stable CO₂ and soft water; it dislikes CO₂ swings and shades itself out if stems are planted too close. Fast once settled.

European native with feathery, light-green comb-like leaves, well suited to cooler and coldwater tanks. Needs strong light — the lower stem rots in shade — and prefers cool water; growth slows and weakens above about 26°C. CO₂ optional.

Fast background stem with narrow leaves that blush pink-red under strong light. Not for low-tech tanks — wants bright light, CO₂ and ample iron and phosphate to colour up. Easy to keep in check by trimming and replanting the tops.

Willow-leaved stem that turns glowing orange-red to pink under strong light and CO₂. Needs intense lighting and good macro/micro dosing for its best colour; greener and leggier otherwise. Trim and replant the tops to keep it tidy.

Easy, affordable stem with rounded bright-green leaves on branching stems. Grows even in low light with no CO₂ — denser and more compact under brighter light. Speeds up dramatically in nutrient-rich tanks. A good beginner midground filler.

Tall crypt with very long, narrow, often crinkled ribbon leaves that make a grassy background thicket. Unusually tolerant of hard, alkaline water. Slow and easy in low light without CO₂; leave it undisturbed after planting to avoid melt.

A rotundifolia x wallichii hybrid with fine needle leaves and golden-green tops, forming dense bushy clumps. Wants strong light to stay compact and colourful; leggy in shade. CO₂ and soft, acidic water bring out its best, with heavy pearling.

Very hardy small-leaved plant that can be grown as a bushy stem, trimmed into a carpet, or left to float. Thrives even in low light with no CO₂, denser and more compact with both. A favourite shrimp and nano plant — fast and forgiving.

Grass-like stem with narrow, slightly translucent green leaves and a soft, flowing look. Fast and undemanding across a wide range of water; medium light and no CO₂ are plenty, though CO₂ makes it fuller. Trim to keep its upright form.

Hardy crypt with broad, heart-shaped leaves that show a reddish underside under brighter light. Genuinely low-light tolerant and undemanding without CO₂. Slow and steady — leave it undisturbed after planting to avoid crypt melt.

Small crypt with narrow, earthy-brown leaves often tinged red, good for the foreground or low midground. Easy in very low light without CO₂; too much light just invites algae on its slow leaves. Spreads gently by runners over time.

Compact, adaptable crypt with olive-green leaves, suiting a foreground in big tanks or a midground in smaller ones. Easy in low light with no CO₂ and a wide range of water; appreciates harder, calcium-rich water. The parent of the 'lutea' form.

Large Anubias with distinctive arrowhead-shaped leaves on long stems, a bold background epiphyte. As tough and slow as the rest of the genus — attach the rhizome to wood or rock, never bury it. Keep it shaded to avoid algae.

Anubias with elegant spade- or triangle-shaped leaves held on tall stalks, taller than most of the genus. Slow, hardy and easy in low light without CO₂. An epiphyte — tie the rhizome to hardscape and leave it exposed.

One of the most popular, beginner-friendly Buces, with narrow, ruffled dark-green leaves. Very slow epiphyte — attach to wood or rock, never bury the rhizome. Easy in low light; bright light only adds algae to its slow leaves.

Tiny Buce whose compact leaves take on red-to-bronze tones, ideal glued to nano hardscape in the foreground. Slow-growing; CO₂ deepens the colour and speeds it a little but isn't required. Keep light modest and parameters stable to avoid melt.

Fine, needle-leaved Ludwigia that flushes orange-red and grows bushy under strong light. Loses its colour and form in low light; a heavy root feeder that wants a rich substrate. The submersed form is delicate and dislikes parameter swings.

Spectacular large stem with whorls of leaves that turn pink, orange and red. A genuine high-tech plant: it needs intense light, injected CO₂, strong flow and full dosing, or the colours fade and the lower leaves drop. Fast and demanding.

A creeping rotala that forms a low foreground mat in pink, red, gold and green under high-tech conditions. Needs strong light, stable CO₂ and good dosing to carpet rather than grow up; trim regularly to keep it dense. An expert carpet.

Variegated form of water wisteria with green-and-cream marbled, deeply cut leaves. Nearly as easy as the plain type — fast and forgiving — but wants brighter light to keep its variegation crisp. Grows well without CO₂.

Easygoing Ludwigia with rounded leaves that take on pink-red tones under brighter light. More forgiving than most reds — grows fast in medium light with no CO₂, redder with both. A good first coloured stem for the midground.

A rotundifolia form prized for holding deep red colour even under modest light — far easier to colour than most reds. Redder still with strong light and iron; greener in low light. A relatively forgiving way to get red into a tank.

Striking stem whose leaves curl and glow coppery-orange to red under bright light. Wants strong lighting, iron and potassium dosing and clean water for its best colour; browns and stalls when underfed. CO₂ optional but helps.

Big, hardy sword with broad heart-shaped leaves that can tower over a tank and even send leaves above the surface. A heavy root feeder — give it a rich substrate and root tabs. Easy in medium light without CO₂, just large; best for big tanks.

Smaller hybrid sword that stays compact enough for the midground, with reddish-brown new leaves under brighter light. Easy and undemanding in medium light without CO₂; colour and compactness improve with more light. A good sword for medium tanks.

Tall bulb plant with dramatic, deeply rippled and embossed dark-green leaves for a bold background. Bury only half the bulb to avoid rot. Tolerant of a wide pH; dislikes big water changes or being moved, and may take a dormant rest.

Elegant bulb plant forming a rosette of long, wavy leaves that turn deep maroon-red under strong light with CO₂ and iron, bronze-green in lower light. Likes an iron-rich substrate and warmth; may go dormant and shed leaves, then regrow from the bulb.

Bulb plant with translucent, arrow-shaped wavy leaves and a graceful look, tolerant of cooler water for temperate tanks. Slow-growing and best with a rich substrate; tends to push some leaves toward the surface. Trim surface pads to keep it submerged.

Fine-leaved val with very narrow, grass-like dark-green blades, daintier than common vallisneria. Spreads quickly by runners into a flowing background curtain. Easy in medium light without CO₂ and tolerant of harder, alkaline water.

Hardy moss with dense, upward-pointing spiky shoots that hold a neat, compact shape on wood and rock. Very forgiving — grows even in low light with no CO₂, fuller and tidier with both. A good, fast moss for beginners.

Low-maintenance moss that drapes in neat triangular fronds, popular for carpeting wood and creating tree shapes. Easy in low to medium light with no CO₂; keep light moderate to avoid algae. Prefers softer, slightly acidic water.

A dense, branching liverwort, not a true moss, that forms a tight dark-green coral-like mat on hardscape. Slow and a little fussy — wants cool, clean, stable water and melts above about 28°C. CO₂ thickens it but isn't essential. Best in a matured tank.

A thick, brittle liverwort with flat, branching dark-green thalli, easy and adaptable across a very wide temperature range. Needs no substrate — tie it down, as it has no roots to anchor itself. Grows slowly without CO₂, denser and faster with it.

A delicate, translucent dwarf fern with finely divided fronds, grown attached to hardscape like a moss. Very slow but tough and undemanding once settled — low light, no CO₂, soft water and gentle flow suit it. Never bury it; tie it to wood or rock.

Compact form of African water fern with finer, more finely-divided fronds that stays smaller than the species. An epiphyte — attach the rhizome to wood or rock, never bury it. Prefers shade, soft slightly-acidic water and gentle flow; CO₂ helps it fill in.

Small floating fern with rounded, fuzzy water-repellent leaves that sit flat on the surface. Very fast and easy across a wide range of conditions — a strong nutrient sponge and good shade for shy fish. Dislikes heavy surface agitation; thin it regularly.

Floating plant with smooth, round bright-green leaves and long trailing roots that shelter fry and shrimp. Easy in low to moderate light with no CO₂ and a wide temperature range. Strong light just makes it grow faster and need more thinning.

Taller, faster relative of parvula hairgrass that carpets the foreground with grassy blades and tolerates cool water for temperate tanks. Needs bright light to stay low and spread, or it stretches up; CO₂ greatly speeds the carpet. Plant in small clumps.

Low water-clover that forms a fine foreground carpet without needing CO₂. Develops small single or clover-like leaves close to the substrate under good light; grows taller in shade. Hardy and undemanding, spreading by runners — a fine low-tech carpet.

Distinctive pennywort whose round leaves are held up on individual stalks in neat whorls, like tiny umbrellas. Needs bright light to stay low and carpet; grows tall and sparse under medium light. CO₂ helps it thrive fully submerged.

Water clover that, under good light, develops the classic four-lobed clover leaf for a charming foreground carpet; in low light it grows taller with only two-lobed leaves. Hardy and easy without CO₂, spreading by runners. Tolerant of cooler water.

Bulb plant with long, narrow, strongly ruffled leaves — like a cross between an Aponogeton and a crinkled crypt. Bury only half the bulb to avoid rot and give it root tabs. Easy in medium light without CO₂; may take a dormant rest.

Low, fine-leaved stem that grows into a neat bushy shrub, useful as a midground accent or a foreground in nano tanks. Stays compact under brighter light; spillier and taller in shade. Easy without CO₂, faster and denser with it. Can even be grown attached to hardscape.

Curiosity stem whose submersed leaves range from simple to feathery and fern-like depending on conditions. Prefers cooler water, so it suits goldfish and temperate tanks. Undemanding without CO₂ in medium light; faster with both. Wants a rich substrate.

Cultivated polysperma with pink-flushed leaves crossed by striking white-to-cream veins, brightest under stronger light. As fast and forgiving as the plain species — grows in medium light with no CO₂, more colourful with both. An easy splash of pink.

Fast background stem with whorls of soft, bright-green needle leaves for a feathery, fluffy look. Wants strong light and does best with CO₂ in soft, slightly acidic water; growth thins in low light. A nutrient-rich substrate keeps it lush.

Tall, fast background hygro with long, narrow willow-like leaves. As tough and easy as the rest of the genus — grows quickly in medium light with no CO₂, lusher with both. Spreads by runners as well as cuttings; great instant background cover.

Fine-leaved rotala that develops green, pink and purple hues under high-tech conditions. Needs strong light, injected CO₂ and full dosing; the lower leaves starve and drop if it isn't trimmed and replanted. A demanding colour stem.

Unusual low, horizontally-creeping Ludwigia that turns orange-red to reddish-brown under strong light, greener in shade. Slower and shorter than most stems, good for the midground. Wants bright light, CO₂ and a rich substrate for its warm colour; dislikes parameter swings.

Striking red water-milfoil with finely divided, feathery leaves in crimson — a rare red among the feathery stems. Demanding: it needs intense light, injected CO₂ and steady dosing to colour up and stay healthy. Strictly a high-tech plant.

Unusual bacopa with fine, milfoil-like feathery green leaves giving an airy texture quite unlike its round-leaved relatives. Grows into thick mats under bright light, thinner without it; CO₂ helps but isn't required. Trim regularly to keep it bushy.

The easiest Pogostemon — whorls of narrow leaves on fast, hardy stems. Unlike its fussier relatives it grows well in moderate light with no CO₂, fuller and more compact with both. A good, undemanding way to get a feathery Pogostemon look.

Narrow-leaved Ludwigia that runs from green in low light and high nitrate to orange-red under bright light and lean dosing. CO₂ gives dense, branching, colourful growth; without it the stems are thinner. A compact coloured midground stem.

Hardy, fast rotundifolia form with many narrow light-green leaves that take on a pink hue under strong light. Easy and tolerant across a wide pH; regrows after trimming without replanting. A reliable green-to-pink background plant for any setup.

Prized crypt with patterned leaves, dark with paler markings and reddish tones. Slow-growing and a touch more demanding than common crypts — wants stable, soft, slightly acidic water and shade. CO₂ helps the colour but isn't required. Leave it undisturbed.

Sought-after crypt whose new leaves emerge soft pink before greening, with pale veining. Slow and best in stable, soft water; CO₂ and a rich substrate bring out more colour and runners. Prone to melt if moved or shocked — give it time to settle.

Large crypt with long, strongly bullate (puckered) green leaves that make a grassy background thicket, often confused with the smaller-leaved usteriana. Tolerant of hard, alkaline water; slow and easy in medium light without CO₂. Don't disturb once planted.

Small, grassy crypt with narrow green leaves that spreads by runners into a low foreground patch. Easy and slow in low to moderate light without CO₂. One of the better crypts for a compact carpet-like foreground; leave it to settle in after planting.

Hardy, undemanding crypt with narrow green leaves for the foreground or low midground. Happy in low light with no CO₂ and draws nutrients from the substrate. Like all crypts it dislikes fast pH swings and a fresh tank — settle it in a cycled aquarium.

Sword with broad leaves marbled cream and green, a bright variegated centrepiece. Needs at least medium light to keep its variegation; a heavy root feeder that wants a rich substrate and root tabs. Easy without CO₂, lusher with it.

Nana form with bright lime-to-golden new leaves that mature greener, a fresh splash on hardscape. As slow and hardy as standard nana — attach the rhizome to wood or rock, never bury it. Easy in low light; keep light modest to limit algae.

Robust Anubias hybrid with deep-green, elongated pointed leaves, good for the midground. Slow, tough and undemanding like the genus — attach the rhizome to hardscape and leave it exposed. Low light and no CO₂ suit it; bright light invites algae.

Rare, prized nana whose leaves are marbled white and green. Wants a bit more light than plain Anubias to keep its variegation — too much and the leaves green over, too little and it loses contrast. Slow epiphyte; attach to hardscape, never bury.

Barteri form with wide, rounded dark-green leaves, a hardy midground epiphyte. Easy across a wide range of light and water without CO₂ — attach the rhizome to wood or rock. Keep light modest, as its slow leaves attract algae under bright lighting.

Popular Buce with dark foliage that shows blue undertones in good light, an easy colourful epiphyte. Very slow — attach to wood or rock, never bury the rhizome. Low light suits it; CO₂ deepens the colour but isn't required.

Compact Buce with dark, slightly wavy leaves that can form a low dark carpet on hardscape. Slow-growing — expect a new leaf every few weeks. Easy in low to medium light; attach the rhizome to wood or rock and keep parameters stable to avoid melt.

Buce with a soft, velvety dark leaf surface and subtle colour shifts, a striking hardscape accent. Slow epiphyte — attach to wood or rock, never bury the rhizome. CO₂ and gentle light bring out the best colour while keeping algae off its slow leaves.

Narrow-leaved corymbosa form that's fast, bushy and very easy — an excellent low-tech background plant. Grows happily in medium light on natural tank CO₂; strong light brings bronze-to-reddish tints to the leaves. A great beginner stem.

Stem clothed in small, round bright-green leaves that can be grown bushy in the midground or trimmed into a loose carpet. Wants fairly strong light to stay compact; leggy in shade. CO₂ speeds and tightens it but isn't essential.

Forgiving, fast lily relative with crinkled, heart-shaped bright-green leaves on individual stalks. A root feeder — give it a rich substrate. Stays compact under medium-to-strong light; in low light it sends leaves to the surface. Trim those to keep it bushy.

Val with tightly spiralled, corkscrew-twisted leaves that make a lively background curtain. Spreads fast by runners; easy in medium light without CO₂ and tolerant of harder, alkaline water. Don't bury the crown when planting.

The most colourful Lindernia — small leaves flush light purple over green stems under strong light. More demanding than rotundifolia: it wants bright light, CO₂, full dosing and soft water to colour and stay compact. A low, vivid foreground accent.

The classic brown/bronze wendtii with wavy-edged mottled leaves, a staple midground crypt. As tough and adaptable as any wendtii — thrives in low light without CO₂ across a wide pH. Slow-growing; leave it undisturbed to avoid crypt melt.

Reddish-brown wendtii form whose colour deepens under stronger light, CO₂ and a rich substrate; greener and taller in low light. Hardy and forgiving either way. A heavy root feeder — give it root tabs in inert substrate.

Variegated wendtii whose leaves carry pink-to-cream streaks against green for a soft pastel look. Wants medium light to keep its variegation; as easy as other wendtii otherwise, low-tech and no CO₂. Settle it in and don't move it.

Easy Sumatran crypt with broad green leaves, good for the midground. Keep light steady — changing the intensity is a common trigger for crypt melt in this species. Low to medium light and no CO₂ suit it; a rich substrate helps.

Uncommon crypt with strongly puckered, embossed leaves that make a dramatic midground accent. Medium-to-bright light brings out the bullate texture; CO₂ and a rich substrate help. Hates parameter swings — settle it in a cycled, stable tank.

Grass-like crypt with long, narrow, gently twisted ribbon leaves for a flowing background thicket. Easy and slow in low to moderate light without CO₂; spreads by runners. Like all crypts, leave it undisturbed after planting.

Hardy crypt with dark-green, quilted leaves with reddish undersides, faster than most once established. Grows well from a rich substrate with low-to-medium light and no CO₂ — in fact CO₂ can trigger melt in this species. Sensitive to sudden changes.

Compact hybrid sword forming a rosette of rusty-red to crimson leaves that green out in low light or with age. Hardy across soft-to-hard water; a heavy root feeder needing a rich substrate. Easy without CO₂, redder and lusher with strong light.

Slower, smaller sword with deep burgundy-to-purple foliage in an upright rosette, good for the midground. Wants strong light plus iron and potassium for its richest colour; CO₂ deepens it further. Greener and drabber when underlit.

Sword with wide, wavy striated leaves ranging from pink to greenish-brown, brighter pink under stronger light. Easy and undemanding without CO₂, given a nutrient-rich substrate for its big root system. A colourful background centrepiece.

Robust sword with long, narrow dark-green leaves, tolerant of cooler water for temperate tanks. Easy in medium light without CO₂, faster with both, and a heavy root feeder wanting a rich substrate. Makes a large background clump.

Striking sword with deep red, slightly wavy leaves that hold their colour only under strong light with iron dosing; CO₂ helps. A heavy root feeder for a rich substrate. A bolder, redder background statement than the common swords.

Distinctive sword whose heart-shaped leaves are held nearly horizontal, fanning outward for an unusual silhouette. Undemanding in medium light without CO₂ given a rich substrate; new leaves often emerge reddish. A characterful midground plant.

Compact sword with narrow, upright lance-shaped leaves whose new growth flushes pink-red. Faster than most swords; bright light, CO₂ and dosing deepen the colour. A nutrient-rich substrate feeds its big root system. A neat coloured midground accent.

Nana form with nearly circular, coin-shaped deep-green leaves, a tidy accent on hardscape. As slow and hardy as any nana — attach the rhizome to wood or rock, never bury it. Easy in low light; keep light modest to avoid algae on its slow leaves.

Anubias with slender, slightly curved lance-shaped leaves, daintier than barteri. Slow, tough and undemanding in low light without CO₂. An epiphyte — attach the rhizome to hardscape and leave it exposed. Tolerant of a wide pH range.

Tall Anubias with long, narrow, leathery green leaves, a hardy background epiphyte. Very slow — can take years to reach full size. Easy in low to medium light without CO₂; attach the rhizome to wood or rock and keep light modest to limit algae.

Rare, delicate nana with almost pure white-and-green leaves. Unusually for an Anubias it wants medium-to-high light and CO₂ to hold its colour and grow, and is prone to melting outside tissue-culture conditions. Attach to hardscape; settle it in gently.

Anubias with long, narrow lance-shaped leaves that make a useful taller background epiphyte. Slow and steady — barely responds to CO₂, so it's a true low-tech plant. Attach the rhizome to wood or rock; keep light low to moderate to avoid algae.

Java fern form with longer, heavily textured and slightly puckered leaves. As hardy and forgiving as standard java fern — attach the rhizome to wood or rock, never bury it. Easy in low light with no CO₂; bright light can scorch the leaves.

One of the smallest Buces, with tiny round leaves that stay tight to the hardscape and can be grown almost moss-like or as a slow carpet. Very slow epiphyte — attach to wood or rock, never bury the rhizome. Easy in low light; CO₂ speeds it up.

Compact Buce with dark green to deep violet, slightly wavy oval leaves that shimmer under light. Very slow epiphyte — attach to wood or rock, never bury the rhizome. Easy in low to medium light; CO₂ deepens the colour but isn't required.

Miniature Buce with very small leaves in deep purplish hues, perfect glued to nano hardscape. Slow-growing — expect a new leaf every few weeks. Easy in low light; keep parameters stable and the rhizome exposed to avoid melt.

Popular Buce with very dark, almost black-green leaves flecked with pale spots like pearls. Slow epiphyte — attach to wood or rock, never bury. Easy in low to medium light; bright light just brings algae onto its slow leaves.

Prized, rare Buce with thick, rugged, strongly toothed dark leaves — one of the most sought-after forms. Very slow even by Buce standards. Attach the rhizome to hardscape; CO₂ and stable conditions help it grow and keep algae off.

Classic Buce whose brown-green leaves take on a blue sheen under good light. Slow-growing epiphyte for wood and rock — never bury the rhizome. Easy in low to medium light; CO₂ enhances the colour and speeds it up but isn't needed.

Distinctive Buce with strongly raised, skeleton-like leaf veins on dark foliage, a collector's favourite. Slow epiphyte — attach to hardscape, never bury. CO₂ and gentle light bring out the texture while keeping algae off its slow leaves.

Compact Buce with narrow dark leaves that flash blue-to-purple iridescence under light. Slow-growing — a new leaf every few weeks. Attach the rhizome to wood or rock; easy in low light, with CO₂ deepening the colour.

Spectacular but finicky stem with fine reddish leaves radiating from the stem, sometimes growing inches per day in ideal tanks. A demanding high-tech plant: it needs intense light, stable CO₂ and very soft water, and quickly sulks when conditions shift.

Large-leaved water star that turns bright pink-to-red under strong light with CO₂ — a bold background plant. CO₂ is essential: without it the lower leaves drop and the colour fades. Fast and hungry, needing good lighting and full dosing.

Rare, colourful Hygrophila with leaves flushing red and pink under bright light. CO₂ is essential — without it the plant turns pale and struggles. A high-tech plant that rewards strong light, stable CO₂ and good dosing with vivid colour.

Deep wine-red repens form that holds its colour well even in modest light — much easier than most reds. Iron dosing keeps the red saturated; CO₂ and bright light make it bushier and even redder. A forgiving, vivid red background stem.

Large stem with finely divided, feathery bright-green leaves that need good light and CO₂ to keep their colour and form. Fast-growing, so it needs frequent trimming. A striking lacy background plant once it has settled into its submersed form.

Thai rotala prized for bright red-to-pink stems that shine under strong light with CO₂. A high-tech colour plant — it needs intense lighting, stable CO₂ and soft water for its best tone, turning greener and leggier without them.

Fan-leaved red-to-purple cabomba, far more demanding than the green species. Wants intense light, soft water and ideally CO₂ to keep its colour and avoid shedding; it withers in low light. A beautiful but tricky high-tech feather stem.

A Cryptocoryne cousin with ovate, slightly ruffled leaves that flush burgundy under good light; olive-green in shade. A heavy root feeder for a rich substrate, wanting iron and potassium for colour. Slow and largely trim-free; CO₂ deepens the red but isn't required.

Large, adaptable crypt-relative with broad spear-shaped leaves that makes a bold background clump. Tolerant of a wide range of water and even fresh tanks; grows submerged or emersed. Easy in medium light without CO₂, given a rich substrate.

Crypt-relative with narrow, slightly wavy leaves and a metallic sheen, a striking midground accent. A heavy root feeder for a nutrient-rich substrate. Slow but hardy in medium light without CO₂; like crypts, it dislikes sudden parameter swings.

Fast, fine-leaved anacharis relative that can be planted or floated, a great nutrient sponge and fry cover. Easy and undemanding in medium light without CO₂, tolerant of cooler water too. Propagates from any fragment; trim to keep it in check.

Dwarf lily that throws up arrow-shaped leaves often mottled purple, easy and adaptable across a wide pH. Will send pads to the surface if left — trim them to keep it bushy below. Hardy in medium light without CO₂; chelated iron keeps the colour.

Tiny-leaved creeping pennywort that forms a fine foreground carpet — but a demanding one. It needs strong light and injected CO₂ with full dosing, decaying in low-tech tanks. Trim often to keep it flat and dense. A high-tech carpet.

Bulb lily whose triangular leaves emerge deep red under brighter light, fading greener in shade. Trim surface pads to keep it submerged and bushy. Easy and adaptable across a very wide pH; chelated iron supports the red colour. CO₂ optional.

Hardy moss with dark-green, slightly keeled leaves that suits cooler and coldwater tanks better than most. Attach it to wood or rock; easy in low light with no CO₂. Prefers cool water and struggles in warm tropical tanks above the mid-20s°C.

Compact moss resembling Christmas moss but tidier and denser, good for moss walls and tree shapes. Attach to wood or rock. Grows in low light but stays most compact and colourful in moderate light with CO₂; trim to keep its shape.

Loose, fast-growing moss with long stringy strands that drape over hardscape. Undemanding and tolerant of a very wide temperature range including coldwater tanks. Easy in low light with no CO₂; vigorous growth means regular trimming.

Larger-leaved duckweed with rounded green fronds and reddish undersides, easier to manage than common duckweed. Fast and easy in low light with no CO₂ across a wide range of water. A strong nutrient sponge and surface cover; thin it regularly.

Large floating plant with glossy paddle leaves on inflated, buoyant stalks and long feathery roots. Wants strong light — ideally near-overhead — to thrive and flower; struggles under typical aquarium hoods. Very fast and a heavy feeder; prune often.

Floating liverwort with heart-shaped green fronds whose undersides are dark and hairy (normal, not rot). Multiplies fast but is easier to control than duckweed, with very short roots that don't block the view. Likes good macronutrients and medium light.

Broad-leaved red macrandra streaked with white veining — beautiful but among the trickiest stems. It needs strong light, very stable CO₂, soft acidic water and careful dosing, stunting at the tips with any CO₂ swing. A connoisseur's plant.

Striking stem with broad pink-to-red leaves crossed by darker veins — a standout colour plant. Unlike most Ludwigia it is demanding: high light, injected CO₂ and full dosing are needed for its colour and form, which fade and stunt without them.

Bold background stem with narrow reddish-brown to gold leaves. Needs strong light reaching the lower stem or it drops its bottom leaves; CO₂ and a rich substrate deepen the colour. Tolerant of harder water but happiest soft and bright.

Deeply red repens form that keeps its colour well even in medium light — an easy red for the background. Iron-rich dosing holds the saturated red; CO₂ and bright light make it bushier still. A forgiving, vivid coloured stem for low-tech tanks.

Distinctive rotala that develops lavender-to-deep-purple tones under strong light with CO₂ — a rare cool-toned colour. Relatively manageable for a colour stem, though it needs good light, CO₂ and nutrients to show its best. Propagates from cuttings.

Easier, narrower-leaved water star with whorls of fine green leaves for a feathery background. Unlike the broad-leaf form it grows well without CO₂ given strong light — leggy and bare-stemmed if underlit. Fast; trim and replant the tops.

Polysperma form with long green leaves marked by darker green veins. As unkillable as the species — grows fast even in low light with no CO₂ across a very wide range of water. A great, foolproof beginner background stem with a bit of pattern.

Vallisneria form whose long ribbon leaves carry dark tiger-stripe speckling. Spreads fast by runners into a flowing background curtain. Hardy and easy in medium light without CO₂ across a very wide pH; a heavy root feeder. Don't bury the crown.

The largest vallisneria, with broad green ribbon leaves that can top a big tank and trail across the surface. Spreads quickly by runners. Easy in medium light without CO₂, tolerant of soft to hard water; needs a rich substrate and a tall tank.

Fast-spreading chain-sword that throws runners into a grassy midground patch or a tall foreground. Easy and undemanding in medium light without CO₂, but its rapid growth means it needs regular micronutrient dosing to avoid pale leaves.

Uncommon crypt with patterned, textured leaves marked in pale and dark green. Slow and best in stable, soft water with a rich substrate. Easy lighting needs (low to moderate) but, like all crypts, sensitive to sudden parameter changes.

The shortest hairgrass, arching at just 3-5 cm to form a tight, lawn-like foreground carpet. Needs bright light to stay low and spread — it stretches up in shade. CO₂ greatly speeds the carpet. Plant in small clumps a couple of cm apart.

Slender-leaved dwarf sagittaria form that spreads by runners into a grassy foreground or low midground sward. Hardy and fast in medium light without CO₂, tolerant of harder, alkaline water. Strong light brings reddish leaf tips; an iron-rich substrate helps.

Easy, fast background stem with small leaves that take on a soft pink-bronze tint under good light. Adaptable across a range of light and water with no CO₂ needed, brighter with both. A reliable, beginner-friendly filler for any tank.

A rotundifolia form from Fujian that intensifies to orange and red under high light with CO₂. As hardy as standard rotundifolia but more colourful when pushed; greener in low light. Fast — regrows after trimming without replanting.

Dwarf, compact rotundifolia form that stays low and bushy for the midground, flushing orange-red at the tips. Wants strong light, CO₂ and lean nitrate for its best colour and tight habit; greener and looser without. Trim often to keep it dense.

Small, slow water fern with finely divided fronds, grown attached to hardscape like a moss. Very slow but tough once settled — low light, soft water and gentle flow suit it; CO₂ speeds it a little. Never bury it; tie it to wood or rock.

Compact narrow-leaf java fern that stays small enough for the foreground or nano hardscape. As hardy and forgiving as standard java fern — attach the rhizome to wood or rock, never bury it. Easy in low light with no CO₂; avoid bright light.

Fast water fern with broader, less finely divided fronds than common water sprite; can be planted or left to float. Very easy in medium light with no CO₂ and a strong nutrient sponge. Floating fronds shade and shelter fry; thin regularly.

Rotundifolia form that develops warm orange-to-peach tops under strong light with CO₂, a softer colour than the reds. As tough and fast as the species, greener in low light. Trim and replant the tops; lean nitrate and good iron keep the colour.

Crypt with broad heart-shaped leaves, often with reddish undersides, from shady streams. Medium light suits it; CO₂ and a rich substrate deepen the colour but aren't required. Slow and, like all crypts, prone to melt if moved or shocked.

Large background crypt with long, bright-green leaves, unusual for tolerating brackish conditions in the wild. Medium-to-bright light and a rich substrate suit it; no CO₂ needed. One of the bigger, faster crypts once it settles in.

Uncommon Bornean crypt with bullate, rusty-toned leaves making a textured midground accent. Slow, and best in stable, soft water with a rich substrate; CO₂ helps it colour and fill in. Low to medium light; sensitive to sudden parameter changes.

Tiny, undemanding crypt with small narrow leaves, suited to a low foreground or nano tank. Easy in low light with no CO₂ across a very wide pH and temperature range. Spreads slowly by runners; leave it undisturbed to avoid melt.

Hardy crypt with narrow, wavy-edged leaves often showing brown-to-bronze tones, a versatile midground plant. Easy in low to medium light without CO₂; brighter light improves colour. A heavy root feeder — keep the crown above the substrate.

Striking crypt whose leaves emerge and stay vivid pink — the colour intensifies under high light with CO₂. Unusually for a crypt it wants strong light to hold the pink. Slow-growing; settle it in stable, soft water and don't move it.

Compact wendtii-type crypt with hammered, brownish-green leaves that stay neat and bushy in the midground. As easy and adaptable as other wendtii — low to medium light, no CO₂ needed. Hardy once established; avoid sudden parameter swings.

Whorled green form of inclinata that pearls heavily under good light. A high-tech plant: it needs strong light, injected CO₂ and full dosing, growing slow and pale without them. Fast and lush when conditions are met; trim and replant the tops.

Caroliniana form whose thick rounded leaves turn pink-to-copper under strong light, greener in shade. As hardy as the species — grows in a wide range of water, slowly, with no CO₂ needed. Strong light and dosing bring out the warm colour.

Hairy-stemmed bacopa with rounded leaves that flush pink-red on top under bright light. Slower and a touch fussier than caroliniana; wants strong light and good dosing for colour, with CO₂ helping. A neat coloured midground bacopa.

Smaller-leaved Hydrocotyle 'Japan' that creeps into a tighter foreground carpet of tiny three-lobed leaves. Needs bright light to stay flat and dense; CO₂ speeds it up. Trim regularly to keep it low and tight rather than climbing.

Vivid red macrandra form, as demanding as the rest of its kind. It needs strong light, stable pressurised CO₂, soft acidic water and rich dosing, stunting at any CO₂ swing. One of the more intense reds for a high-tech midground.

Dwarf, compact super red that stays low enough for the midground and holds blood-red colour even in medium light. More forgiving than most reds; CO₂ and bright light make it bushier and redder. A tidy, easy red accent for smaller tanks.

Selected rotundifolia that reddens more readily than the standard plant, given strong light and lean nitrate. As tough and fast as any rotundifolia, greener in low light. A reliable, inexpensive way to get red into the background; trim often.

Anubias with broad, slightly heart-shaped leaves on longer stems, a hardy midground epiphyte. Slow and easy in low light without CO₂. Attach the rhizome to wood or rock, never bury it; keep light modest so algae stays off its slow leaves.

Large Anubias whose long leaves can exceed 30 cm in good conditions, a bold background epiphyte for big tanks. As slow and tough as the genus — attach the rhizome to hardscape, never bury it. Low light, no CO₂; can flower underwater.

Miniature nana with fine white-and-green vein patterning that gives a speckled, starry look. As hardy as petite but pricier — attach to nano hardscape, never bury the rhizome. Easy in low light; keep light modest to preserve the variegation.

Buce with broad, rounded apple-shaped green leaves, larger than most forms. Slow-growing epiphyte — attach to wood or rock, never bury the rhizome. Easy in low to medium light; CO₂ and gentle light keep it healthy and algae-free.

Buce with a soft, matte velvety leaf surface and dark colour, a refined hardscape accent. Slow epiphyte — attach to wood or rock, never bury. CO₂ and subdued light bring out the best colour while keeping algae off its slow leaves.

Popular, easy Buce with narrow wavy dark-green leaves that take on subtle blue-purple tones. Slow-growing epiphyte for hardscape — never bury the rhizome. Easy in low to medium light; CO₂ speeds it and deepens colour but isn't required.

Compact nana cultivar with rounded leaves bearing pale eye-like spots. Slow, hardy and easy in low light without CO₂, like the rest of the genus — attach the rhizome to nano hardscape, never bury it. Keep light modest to hold the pattern.

Curious hairgrass that sprouts plantlets at the tips of its blades, which arch over and root to spread. Wants bright light to stay compact and green; leggy in shade. CO₂ speeds it. A grassy, characterful background or tall midground plant.

Forms a low foreground carpet of short, fleshy, fat blades by runners. Slow-growing; tolerates low light but needs brighter light to stay short and lawn-like rather than stretching up. Suits cooler tanks. CO₂ helps it carpet faster.

Grass-like foreground carpet of short narrow blades that spreads by runners into a dense lawn. Needs bright, direct light to carpet rather than grow tall; CO₂ thickens it considerably. A nutrient-rich substrate helps it establish.

Small creeping carpet plant, even finer than common glosso. A high-tech carpet — it needs strong light and injected CO₂ to hug the substrate, growing upward and loose in low light or low CO₂. Trim regularly to keep the mat flat and dense.

Floating fern with chains of small, fuzzy, water-repellent leaves that sit flat on the surface. Fast and easy in medium light with no CO₂, and a strong nutrient sponge against algae. Dislikes heavy surface agitation; thin it regularly.

Water clover with narrow, grass-like submersed leaves that forms a fine foreground carpet without needing CO₂. Develops the classic clover leaf in the right conditions; taller and looser in low light. Hardy and undemanding, spreading by runners.

Fast, easy background hygro with long bright-green leaves. As tough as the genus — grows in a wide range of light and water with no CO₂, denser and tidier with both. Drops lower leaves if shaded; trim and replant the tops to keep it bushy.

Vigorous, very adaptable background hygro tolerant of a huge range of light, temperature and pH. Grows in low light with no CO₂, faster and lusher with both. A fast, foolproof background filler; prune frequently to keep it from taking over.

Bushy stem with serrated spear-shaped leaves, one of the few aquarium daisies and a strong nutrient sponge against algae. Fast and easy in medium light with no CO₂, lusher with both. Frequent trimming keeps it dense; replant the cuttings.

Unusual floating Ludwigia that mats across the surface on spongy white aerial roots, the leaves rounding and reddening under strong light. Easy with no CO₂ but wants bright, near-overhead light for its colour and dense growth. A good surface nutrient sponge.

Feathery ambulia relative with whorls of finely divided bright-green leaves for a soft background bush. Grows well in medium light, fuller and more compact with CO₂; throws horizontal side shoots when bushy. Easy and fast — trim regularly.

Bacopa with rounded, slightly scalloped light-green leaves, a fresh easy midground stem. Grows in a wide range of conditions with no CO₂, fuller with strong light and dosing. Hardy and undemanding; pinch the tips for bushier growth.

Golden-leaved moneywort with round coin-shaped leaves that glow lime-to-yellow under strong light, greener in shade. Prefers cooler water and bright light to keep its colour and stay compact. Easy with no CO₂; tolerant of temperate tanks.

Tall background stem prized for narrow leaves that flush pink-to-orange under intense light. A demanding high-tech plant — wants strong light and pressurised CO₂; without them growth slows and the colour fades to a muddy green. Plant in groups for a glowing backdrop.

Fine-leaved midground stem that turns scarlet on the upper surface of its leaves under bright light. Not strictly CO₂-dependent but much fuller and redder with it and soft water. Trim regularly for a bushy mound; lower leaves shed if shaded.

Compact rosette of bright-green spoon-shaped leaves arranged like a rose, a striking foreground accent. Wants strong light and cool water; tends to deteriorate fully submerged over time, so it shines in cold-water, brackish or paludarium setups. Slow and undemanding for CO₂.

Eriocaulon relative forming a rosette of fine needle-like leaves on a creeping stem, a delicate aquascaping showpiece. One of the harder plants to keep: demands very soft, acidic water, strong light and steady CO₂. Reward for stable, precise conditions.

Unusual stem with whorls of very fine thread-like leaves giving a soft, feathery background bush. A fast grower without high demands, but it stretches and browns in weak light — give it strong light, and CO₂ and nutrients for the densest foliage.

Tiny rosette that runners into a bright-green lawn much like a chain sword, spreading fast under strong light. Wants bright, unshaded foreground placement and benefits from CO₂ and a rich substrate to carpet tightly. Trim runners to keep it dense.

Grass-like rosette of long ribbon leaves, taller than its cousin B. japonica and a soft midground bush. Prefers soft, slightly acidic water and gentle flow — fine leaves bruise in strong current. Bright light plus CO₂ deepen colour and density.

Tiny succulent-leaved stem that mats into a low foreground bed, extremely tolerant of cold and a wide pH. Thrives in cool-water tanks with bright light and no CO₂. Note: an aggressive invasive outside its range — never release it into the wild.

Sturdy marsh stem with thick rounded leaves, green above and purple-red beneath, reddening under strong light. Slow but easy and forgiving — grows in medium light with no CO₂, faster and redder with both. Tolerates cooler, temperate tanks well.

Small-leaved lindernia whose narrow leaves are speckled and edged cream-white for a fine variegated midground stem. Holds its variegation best under moderate-to-strong light; fuller and tidier with CO₂. Pinch the tips for bushier growth.

Classic narrow-leaf chain sword that spreads by runners into a grassy foreground carpet. Hardy and undemanding — needs only a nutrient substrate and medium light, no CO₂ required. Moderate-to-strong light keeps it low and compact rather than leggy.

Distinctive Limnophila with broad, hammered basil-like leaves on a stout stem, very different from the feathery ambulias. Undemanding and slow-growing in medium light; fuller in soft water with CO₂ and good dosing. A handsome textural background plant.

Floating Ludwigia that arranges diamond-shaped leaves into a flat rosette like a mosaic tile on the surface, reddening under strong light. Wants bright, near-overhead light and a rich water column; intolerant of alkaline water above pH 8. A spectacular surface centrepiece.
